text: Well, good day everyone and welcome to the Oracle Corporation fourth quarter fiscal year
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: 2026 earnings call.

text: Just a reminder, this call is being recorded.

text: If you have a question today, please press star one on your telephone keypad.

text: Please limit your questions to one.

text: I would now like to hand the conference over to Mr. Ken Bond.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Please go ahead, sir.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you, Lisa.
speaker: KEN BOND - SVP, Investor Relations, Oracle

text: And good afternoon, everyone.
speaker: MIKE SICILIA - CEO, Oracle

text: Welcome to Oracle's fourth quarter and fiscal year 2026 earnings conference call.
speaker: MIKE SICILIA - CEO, Oracle

text: On the call today are Chief Executive Officer Mike Cecilia, Chief Executive Officer Clay
speaker: MIKE SICILIA - CEO, Oracle

text: McGurk, and Chief Financial Officer Hillary Maxson, a copy of the press release, including
speaker: MIKE SICILIA - CEO, Oracle

text: financial results, tables, supplemental financial metrics and guidance are now available from
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: the investor relations website.

text: Also is the slide deck being introduced this quarter, which you'll see momentarily, a
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: gap to non-gap reconciliation, other supplemental financial information, and list of many customers
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: who purchased Oracle Cloud Services or went live on Oracle Cloud recently.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: These items will be available after today's call.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: As a reminder, today's discussion will include forward-looking statements and we will make
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: some important comments around factors relating to our business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: These forward-looking statements are also subject to risks and uncertainties that may cause
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: actual results to differ materially from statements being made today.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: As a result, we caution you against placing undue reliance on these forward-looking statements
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and we encourage you to review our most recent reports, including our 10K and 10Q and any
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: applicable amendments.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And finally, we are not obligating ourselves to revise our results for these forward-looking
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: statements in light of new information or future events.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Before taking any questions, we'll begin with a few prepared remarks.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And with that, I'll turn the call to Hillary.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thanks, Ken.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Hi, everyone.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Great to be here with you today.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And as soon as CSO, I thought I'd start with the few thoughts on why I'm so excited
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: to join Oracle at this time.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I've set my career all around the world by companies that use technology and data to
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: drive transformation, both internally and for customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And I believe that the most valuable transformational change sits at the juxtaposition of the physical
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and virtual worlds across business models from infrastructure to enterprise software.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Oracle understands that intersection and is now uniquely positioned for one of the most
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: significant technology transitions we've seen in decades.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Very few companies can help customers across the entire technology stack, from the cloud
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: infrastructure that powers AI workloads to the mission-critical applications that run
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: their businesses.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Oracle can do both.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Plus, this is a company with deep technical expertise, differentiated technology, and
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: a long history of helping customers turn technology innovation into tangible business
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: value.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And now I've only been here for two months.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Everything I've seen has reinforced my confidence in the company's strategy, execution, and
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: opportunity ahead.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I'm excited to be part of the team and look forward to helping Oracle capitalize on the
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: opportunities in front of us to drive return on investment and shareholder value.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And as we pursue these opportunities, we'll remain focused on disciplined capital allocation,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: maintaining a strong balance sheet, and preserving our investment-grade credit rating.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: With that, let me turn to our Q4 and fiscal year 26 results.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And like Ken said, we've introduced a short presentation to accompany our earnings calls,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: so you can follow along with the numbers and key comments we'll make today.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In terms of Q4, it was a record quarter, driven by strengths in both our cloud infrastructure
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and cloud apps business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Revenue was $19.2 billion, up 21% in US dollars.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: My infrastructure revenue grew 93%, reflecting strong demand for both AI workloads and our
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: database services, and cloud apps was up double-digit at plus 10%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And my play will give more detail on these businesses in just a moment.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our non-gasp operating income increased 22% in US dollars to $8.6 billion, driven by our
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: strong revenue progression.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our operating margin increased slightly, with our gross margin declining, driven by
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: impacts from ramping up our data centers, and the acceleration in our infrastructure revenue.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: This was more than offset in the quarter by a reduction in operating costs, and for us,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that's the lines in our P&L, starting with sales and marketing, due to efficiency actions
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in our cost structure.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our non-gasp ETS reached $2.11, an increase of 24% in US dollars to the quarter, due to
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: a one-time net gain on investment.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Excluding this, our non-gasp ETS increased by 20%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Turned into the full year, we surpassed revenues of $67 billion for the first time, which translated
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: into strong, non-gasp operating income of 29 billion, up 16% in US dollars for the
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: year.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our non-gasp ETS was up 27% in US dollars to $7.63, including one-time gains on investment.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Excluding these gains, our non-gasp ETS was $6.83.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In the full year, our gross margin stepped down around five points, as expected, as we
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: start to see the impacts from the build-out of our infrastructure business, and the acceleration
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in its revenues, primarily offset by lower operating costs as a percentage of revenue,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: driven by operating efficiencies.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: All of this translated into strong cash flow from operations of $32 billion, up 54%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We did continue with our program of capital investments tied to unlocking the strong growth
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: opportunities in front of us.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our net cash outlay for capital expenditures for the full year was $48 billion, taking
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: into account free payments and timing and facts of around $8 billion.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You can set the table showing the details of net cash outlay for capex in our press release.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We think this measure is important to better understand our funding needs.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And our remaining performance obligations, or RPO, finished at $638 billion of 363%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: This unprecedented level of RPO provides exceptional visibility into our future revenue growth,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: all supported by long-term contractual customer commitments, and reflects the strong customer
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: demand we see across both AI infrastructure and cloud services.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: To give a bit more detail on our RPO, we expect 12% to be recognized in the next 12 months,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and another 34% between 13 and 36 months.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And these percentages are both expected to accelerate over the coming quarters based
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: on our current long-term outlook.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: My complaint will now get into a bit more detail on our cloud businesses, and then I'll
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: be back with our outlook for fiscal year 27 and 21.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you, Hillary, and welcome to Oracle.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So I'm going to cover our cloud apps and cloud database business in a bit more detail,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: both have been performed quite well in P4.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We're on the front of one of the most interesting times in the technology business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our customers are now focused on how to leverage AI in their own businesses.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: They want AI to increase productivity, enhance customer service,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and create real content and advantages.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: They go through it quickly and within their existing budget novel.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Oracle's UND advantage is that we deliver the applications, the data, the infrastructure,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: the AI tooling, and the industry expertise together.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: That combination invariably puts us at the center of customer conversations,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: whether they're assisting Oracle customers or not.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And our customers have moved past the experiment stage of AI.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: They are ready to implement enterprise-free, complete agentic solutions to help run their businesses.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Over the past year, we have delivered more than 1,000 AI agents across our application suites.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: These agent-based offerings can reason, decide, and execute work across processes.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So the quickest, most affordable, and most productive way customers can begin consuming AI
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: is just to continue using Oracle's applications.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Since every three months, they get more and more of the AI features built for them and ready to go.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: This is a major shift in enterprise software, and Oracle is uniquely positioned to lead it.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And you can see it in our Q4 results, Oracle Cloud Applications,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: generated revenues of $4.1 billion, which is up 10%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And our stats of third revenue was up 16% in the quarter.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Across the company, we took thousands of customers' live less order,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: over 300 infusion alone.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Exelarn adopted our utility platform to manage operations.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Wright County Sheriff's Office went live with our public safety suite.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Westfield Insurance implemented Fusion ERP,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and Myrais Bank went live with the Oracle banking, just to name a few.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: All of these customers are upgrading to a better and modern applications platform
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that also comes with AI built right in.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In Q4, we also continue our electronic health record deployment
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: at the United States Department of Veterans Affairs.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In Q4, we added four VA medical centers in Michigan,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and in early June added another four VA medical centers in Ohio.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The Oracle now supports 14 VA medical centers serving 29,000 clinicians
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and 500,000 veterans across the United States.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Okay, well, on our part of our Q4 bookings,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: the United States government office of personnel management today
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: announced an agency-wide award to Oracle Perfusion ACM.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So this is obviously a strong start for us in our ECFLY 27 applications business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In addition to discussions around AI within our applications,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I'm also having very interesting conversations with our customers
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: around leveraging their own proprietary datasets with AI.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Much of this data already sits in an Oracle database
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: or is generated by Oracle applications.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: For many enterprises,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: referencing against decades of rich operations data
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: is where the benefits of AI compound exponentially.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Oracle's full stack offerings allow customers to get up and running quickly,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: leveraging AI together with their private datasets.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: This is like Claro, a major telecommunications provider in Latin America
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: chose OCI, field services applications,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and our AI data platform to automate customer service
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: for their 30 million subscribers this quarter.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: UK National Health Services share business services,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: oldest of Brazilian retailers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and QXO, the fastest growing building products distributor in the United States,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: combined AI-ready Oracle infrastructure,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: or database products with Oracle applications
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: to move their businesses forward.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Again, just to name a few.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Last quarter, we also released a long list of major new AI functionality
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in the Oracle database.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Here are just two examples.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The Oracle AI agent memory is a library that helps developers build agents
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that can remember, reason, and act with enterprise context.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Oracle deep data security adds data access goals at the database level.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: This protects against both unauthorized access
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and limits precisely what data a user and any AI agent
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: acting on their behalf can see where active on.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: All of these innovations I've just described and many more are available
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in our cloud, our partners' clouds, and in our customers' environments.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In Q4, our cloud database business revenue grew by 29%,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: but mostly cloud growing much faster.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Both cloud revenue was up 404% year over year,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and bookings were up to 325% year over year.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: One example of an enterprise using a wide range of Oracle technologies in Bota-Poh,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: who turned to less than Q4 to consolidate and modernize their operations.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Bota-Poh selected OCI-identicated region and their data centers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: our multi-cloud database offering and a partner cloud,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and our applications to reduce costs and run their processes faster,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in some cases, up to 16% faster.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Finally, we are working with our customers to deliver quick ROI within their AI budgets.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: To do so, we are simplifying how customers consume and pay for agentability capabilities.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our new agent pricing aligns with customer value.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Now, much of our AI innovation in our core applications continues to be included
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: at no extra charge.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: However, customers can also purchase additional agent capacity
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in a simple, predictable way by purchasing bundles of tokens
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that can be used across their application suites.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We're also introducing outcome-based commercial models
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that align pricing directly to the value derived.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: For example, interview agents that are priced based on the number of candidates' rate,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: or hospitality of sale agents priced on the percentage of end consumer of sale transactions.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In Q4, we started a limited rollout of our token bundles and had 33 customers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: like AON Services Corporation and Liberty Energy,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: repurchase tokens that have access to more advanced reasoning in models.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: All of this helps our customers control their costs
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and align their spending with the value being generated.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And with that, I will turn it over to Clay.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thanks, Mike.

text: OK, you just heard from Mike about our applications and database businesses.

text: Oracle has been in need of businesses for decades,

text: and they continue to impress us because of their ability to continually grow
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: aggregate margins of dollars through a combination of durable differentiation
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and increase in market size.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I want to share how we see our infrastructure business in that same category
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and the evidence that enforces that in-depth.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: OK, differentiation comes in many forms.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Technological innovation, supply chain execution,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: operational ability in-work.

text: We created OCI as the most highly secure, highest performance,

text: and lowest cost networks.

text: to implement incredibly efficient and flexible supply chains.

text: We architect across data center design, power distribution, data

text: whole layout, and networking to deliver the most efficient

text: and the most flexible infrastructure available to you where.

text: Oracle had a long track record of durable differentiation.

text: This is because we know the real differentiator is the organization,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: the people, the company itself that can adapt to new requirements,

text: invent solutions, and deliver them to customers wrapped.

text: And now with AI infrastructure, we've shown to everyone

text: the power of the organization we built, the technology we've created,

speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And our strong Q4 results reflect this well.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Customer demand and our growing visibility into future revenues
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: is what underpins the long-term financial outlook we shared at our most recent analyst day
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: of plus 31% revenue taker and plus 28% EPS taker through our fiscal year 2030.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In order to unlock this unique growth opportunity,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: we started a program of capital investments.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We'll continue those investments in our fiscal year 2027
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: with an expected net cash outlay for capital expenditures of around $70 billion.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: This includes customer prepayments and timing impacts
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: expected at around $20 to $25 billion.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So our reported capex will be higher by this amount.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Importantly, these investments are being driven by committed customer demand
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: reflected in our record RPO, giving us confidence in our long-term outlook
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: as well as strong returns on the capital we're deploying.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: As Clay already mentioned, this demand is allowing us to garner customer prepayments
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and bring your own hardware at similar or better markings than the rest of our contracts.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: To support our capital investments program,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: we expect to raise around $40 billion in debt and equity in our fiscal year 27
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and that includes our already announced $20 billion at the market equity issuance.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We don't anticipate raising additional debt funding in calendar year 2026.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: To our fiscal year 2027 guidance, you can start to see the strong translation
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: of our RPO into revenues.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: With expected growth in our total revenues of plus 34% in constant currency,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: surpassing the five-year revenue taker included in our long-term outlook.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our fiscal year 2027 gross margins will step down due to timing for the ramp up
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: of our data center project into their full revenue contribution, plus impacts from mix.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: While these investments are creating pressure on the near term to gross margins
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in our infrastructure business, we expect margin performance and infrastructure
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: to improve rapidly as we reach full contractual revenue levels at our data centers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Operating costs, we expect to be slightly negative year over year
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in dollar terms due to efficiency actions driving improved operating leverage.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: NetNet, we expect our non-GAS BTS for the year to be $8.05, up 18% in constant currency,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: excluding the net one-time investment gains we booked in fiscal year 26
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: from ampere and bloom energy.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Offedage with guidance for our Q1 2027.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In Q1, we'd expect growth in total revenues of between 27 and 29% in US dollars.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Of that, we expect growth in cloud revenues of between 58 and 64%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In non-GAS BTS, we expect between $1.72 and 176, up between 17 and 20% in US dollars.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And anticipate revenues and earnings will accelerate in the second half of the year
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: as we bring further megawatts online at our data centers to fulfill customer demand.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I look forward to speaking further with all of you over the next few weeks and months leading into our Q1
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and at our next Oracle Investor Day scheduled for October 28th in Las Vegas.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: With that, I'll turn the call back to Ken for the Q&A.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you, Hillary. Lisa, if you please pull the audience for any questions they might have.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Absolutely, and ladies and gentlemen, once again, that is star one.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: If you have a question, we ask that you limit your questions to one.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The first question comes from John D'Fuchee from Guggenheim Securities.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So my question is a question that I've dealt with all this quarter.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In Clay, that was a ton of information you gave, which is helpful, really helpful.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But there's one little nuance here.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You spend a little bit more of this quarter on CAPX than we expected.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And that's sort of the topic a little bit that adds into it.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We all know that component costs have gone up a lot, especially memory,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: grown significantly, right?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And even though you said that most 3Q and 4Q contracts are large-scale AI contracts
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that were prepaid for GPUs, you have a lot of other contracts.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You know, this has been an issue for a lot of software companies
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and large cloud companies.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I don't think it's as much of an issue for you given my understanding
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: of how you construct your contracts.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But can you explain that to investors, like when it comes to these very long-term contracts,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: like between you and the end customer and the suppliers?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Sure, yeah. Good question, John.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Good to talk to you again, as always.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But I'll answer any of that in two parts.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In terms of the capital expenditures, at least from what we're seeing in Q4,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: any increase in CAPX that is not due to component prices from our perspective,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that's largely around timing, right?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I mean, part of my job is to figure out ways to actually accelerate CAPX.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: It's till we're in tough life.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: My job is to try to spend the money a little bit faster so I can give RAM revenue sometimes.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So I don't see that in the way that the component prices.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Now, talking about component prices in general, look, I think everyone knows that memory prices
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: have definitely gone up as a seed price, as hard drive prices, et cetera.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So one of the things that we do, John, is it's actually quite simple.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: When we're selling stuff in a time period where we have certainty,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: whether that be certainty because the capacities are either sold or we have certainty
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: because we have lost prices across the spectrum,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: whether it be space and power costs, energy costs, people costs, component costs.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: When we know those costs, we will then do fixed price contracts.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Sometimes we don't know those costs because it's out too far in the future
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: or we have too much supply chain risk, whether that be due to just the way the world works
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: or a lack of things being locked in.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We then do not do fixed price contracts with our customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And we have a mechanism whereby those costs end up being flipped.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So, you know, I don't like it when costs go up.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Our customers don't like it when costs go up.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And honestly, I don't think our suppliers do.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I think they'd love to be able to deal with everything we want.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But when the costs do go up, we have a, I think, a very robust set of mechanisms
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that ensure that Oracle is not sitting there with reduced margins.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: That is really helpful. It makes a ton of sense.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And if I could, just a quick one, Hillary kind of alluded to what I'm going to ask,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: but this is a second question I get a ton of questions on.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You have a long-term targets out there.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You're a new CFO, right? And congrats.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: It's great to have you on the line.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But can you just comment on those long-term targets at all?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I know you've only been there a couple of months.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You know, I think, and that would be the intention of putting it in the slide,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I think that we're reconfirming those long-term targets in the sense of the cakers
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: that we put into the slide today.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So, we feel comfortable with that.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And you can see the RPO building to the level that you can start to have a lot of belief, I think,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in those long-term targets, exactly.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So, full re-confirmation from my slide on the long-term targets.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: All very clear. Thank you very much. Nice job, guys.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The next question comes from Brad. Does that all next to what you think?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Great. Thank you so much for taking the question.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And Hillary, welcome to Oracle.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Hillary, as you've come to Oracle from a capital intensive business in another industry,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: how would you suggest that investors evaluate Oracle's progress
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and returns during this period of heavy investment?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Yeah, so the way I think about it, and as we said in the earnings call,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: we feel the returns for the infrastructure business, so that CPU and GPU business are quite strong.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Probably from a back-event envelope standpoint,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: the way I'd think about returns from that business model is in return on invested capital.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And what we see is return on invested capital in the high 20s at a steady state.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So, once the revenues have ramped for large projects at the project level,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and that doesn't take into account up sides like who knows if the GPUs don't need to be replaced over the long-term
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and things like that. Just purely in the steady state,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: when we're at the steady state of the contract that we have.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And as we're generally able to preserve and improve margins,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in the case of things like bring your own hardware, the ROIC structures,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: the ROIC for those types of structures will be even higher.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And again, that back-up envelope, I'm just calculating return on invested capital
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: as after-tax operating margin plus depreciation divided by gross investment.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So, total gross cap eggs at the project level.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Maybe that gives you a little bit of an idea.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And of course, we're happy to talk more about that over the next couple quarters.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: That's really helpful, Vilarie. Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And congrats to the whole team on the execution this quarter.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Nice shop to everybody. Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Up next, we'll take a question from Mark Mourdler from Bernstein.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you very much for taking my question and also congrats on the quarter.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And Hilary, welcome. And we're really looking forward to working with you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Clay and Hilary, with so many vendors entering the market to deliver AI data centers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: including the NEO clouds, SpaceX, which is now going to build data centers in space, etc.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Where does Oracle see itself in the competitive landscape?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And how do you see that increase in capacity impacting your ability to one retain customers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: renew contracts to capture new customers and three maintain or improve margins?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Yeah, thanks. Thanks, Mark.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Look, I think that first, I think it's very important that we stay focused on customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So the nice thing is that, think whether you see it from existing RPO or increased contracts that we're getting.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Yes, there's a lot of things happening in the market, but we have a large diverse set of customers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: both very large and also smaller customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And what I spend all of my time doing is I wake up every day and I go,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: how do I make sure those customers are as happy as possible to us?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And that's, you know, when I share the numbers, for example,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: in my, in the, in the prepared remarks about the extremely high utilization rate,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: even when things come back for renewal, they're instantly snapped up.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Those are all indicators that we have great customer relationships.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: They're happy with the products and they're very satisfied with the prices that we're charging for them.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But I think there's going to be a lot of people who've been in the space.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I think there's clearly, you know, several years in,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: there's still a massively higher demand than there is supply.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And so I think that's going to, there are going to be more and more people trying to figure out how to meet that demand.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But I don't worry about that. I really focused on how to remake sure that we can meet as much of that demand at a regional margin profile.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And that's what I think you've seen us invent new business models to go out and try to serve.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: In terms of how does that affect our future renewals,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I find that largely what affects future renewals is the several years relationship that we're going to have between now and then.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And we're fundamentally in the service business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: It's one, if you think that you're just buying something and then you're done with it, it's not the way it works, right?
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: These people are relying on what we do in Oracle to run and maintain these massive clusters every day.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And our ability to do that extremely well creates an extremely positive relationship that then ensures that the newals go well.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And then in terms of the margin profile, look, you know, I've been in Oracle now for 12 years.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The whole time I've been working on OCI.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: What I can tell you is it's not easy to build an extremely efficient, highly secure, robust cloud.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So I think that our customers see and appreciate the value of what we provide.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The flexibility that we give them, the comprehensive set of services that we provide.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So I think that over time as, you know, the market continues to mature and we deploy more and more of our research and development dollars into making more efficient.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: I think there's ways that Oracle gets higher and higher margins, but we actually can offer lower and lower prices to our customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: That's ultimately the job that is on our shoulders and what we've been doing over the past decade and it's like the biggest and most robust customers come our way.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: That's really helpful. I do really appreciate it.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The next question today is Keith Bachmann from Bank of Montreal.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Yes, thank you very much for the question. Mike, I wanted to direct this to you. If I could.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You mentioned two things as net new one was moving towards outcome based commercial pricing models.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The other was rolling out some incremental token packages and I wanted to see if you could flush out the wide and more specifically on the commercial outcome based commercial pricing models.

text: How do you think this reduces friction and what is this related to what modules in other words, I assume this is the SAS portfolio ERP, ACM, what models might this relate to.

text: And then finally, how do you think this might impact Brooks? That's it for me. Many thanks.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Sure. Yeah, thanks for the question.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So, so, so, I was just pressing is not entirely new for us. So this is something we've been doing in our construction business based upon construction value under management subcontractor, a general contractor, a subcontractor cash flow and payments.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Also, Leo, as I mentioned, I prepared marks with hospitality and even in healthcare in our new AI based automated agents where we're automating doctors notes for automating lab owners, we're able to measure and actually price based on patients.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Which is, which is what the providers, one of the things providers fear about is how many people can we get through a healthcare system reduce weight and cues to be better serviced patients.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: What's doing, what is you, is that we're now expanding that offering across our entire fleet. And as you mentioned, across all of our applications, including, including, including our future fees.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Now, the, the sort of difficulty is if you're not created the outcome in the first place, that's, that's a tricky thing, a tricky thing to press in. But since we made this full stack invested, it seems we're able to very easily take, you know, the best of the output from the large language models who are our customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And so, we have a very, very, very very, very very good outcome that's a very good outcome for our customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And as I mentioned, you know, one of the things we're increasingly here as customers is how much are we going to spend on, on AI and how do I get ROI very quickly.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So I think we have a very unique advantage since we're in the infrastructure business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: a large, along the vendor's training on there.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We've got all of our applications,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: business of the horizontal and vertical businesses.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We're naturally generating these outcomes for customers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and it really gives us the ability
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: to help them understand their own AI budgets,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: as well as align that to the value,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: again, which is really easy to measure.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So I think it's a unique offering.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: It relies on the full stack investment that we made.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And as I mentioned, early days,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: but certainly resonating very, very, very, very customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: They appreciate the transparency.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: They appreciate being able to align outcomes to AI spec.

text: I also mentioned the token bundles.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Customers want this again.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: A lot of what we're doing are fusion applications,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: are industry applications.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: We can see them to add at no additional charts.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: The customers want access to advanced reasoning,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and they want it, they want it, they want to, you know,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: essentially, for more tokens at the models,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: we have free package bundles to allow them to do that.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So we're allowing as much flexibility,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and as much aligned with the value in our pricing models,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: across our entire applications as we possibly can.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And I expect that that will continue to resonate well with customers

text: as it did in the quarter,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and as we roll it out across our entire fleet,

text: you know, certainly should be helpful for our growth story as well.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: Thanks, Mike.

text: we know all of us, how things are going to change in the future.

text: Right? You have a lot of different types of accelerators. You have a lot of different customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You have a lot of different business and arrangements. And so, you know, ultimately,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: one of the things that Oracle can provide to our customers is that we can go out and put up front capital
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: and then depreciate that over a period of time and help finance the customer reduces to that.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: But that's not the only thing we provide and for a lot of customers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: it's not even the most important thing to provide.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: What they contract with us for is the ability to go out and get these data centers constructed,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: design them properly, secure them, design networks that go inside of them, install a cloud,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: give the complimentary set of services around the specific hardware because it turns out that
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: instead of the accelerators on their own is not functioning cloud. You need general purpose compute,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: general purpose storage, you need load balancers, you need security functions, you need identity.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You need all of that to actually make this stuff usable and Oracle provides all of that.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: And then anyone that thinks that these things are easy to operate is very confused.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: You're not just buying a single rack and putting it into your data hall.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: These are extremely complex clusters that require constant care and feeding,
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: constant maintenance across the network and the hardware itself.

text: And so when you add all that together, I think that what you're seeing is that you've got different entrance in the market
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: around accelerators that are helping customers find ways to procure their accelerators.

text: And you've got different customers, you have different ways of thinking about that.

text: So let me just start.

speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: requirement when we look at how we plan our business.

text: And also from an economic standpoint, of course, because we're collecting money up front.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa

text: So in normal cases, we would put out the capex amount and then later we would collect money from customers.
speaker: OPERATOR - Lisa
